speaker: interviewer
text: December 22, 1995. Kurt Greenhut, tape four. Mr. Greenhut, we left off that you were teaching high school history and German in Asbury Park. How long did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Asbury Park, I only stayed for one year, but then I went on to Pennsylvania and at Bristol Town-- taught at Bristol Township School System for eight years. And, and then finally--

speaker: interviewer
text: At what level did you teach at Bristol Township?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Bristol Township, world history, and American government, American history. You know, so world history, American gov-- history, and American government. And then I went, I got-- I got a job at Princeton High School, Princeton High School-- well, Princeton Middle School in 1964.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what state is that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: New Jersey, so in other words, I was able to-- I ended my career in New J-- in As-- in Princeton, New Jersey,

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you teach in Princeton, New Jersey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if '64, so I finished in 1990. So I'd been there for quite a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what caused you to stop teaching?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's that? Oh, well, I, I got sick. You know, in other words, I have a heart problem. So the cold weather was bothering me. And so I had-- Lil and I had to make a choice and our choice was to move down here to a warmer climate and that's where I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what was your wife doing at that time? You mentioned she was the secretary at a school. Did she do anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Eventually, she beca-- she worked for a law firm, and then she-- law firm in Trenton, New Jersey. And then she moved on to, to Mathematica, a computer company. And then Martin Marietta and, and Online, all these are in computer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in any other activities, say vocational activities, besides teaching in these various school systems that you've alluded to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, I, I also became-- I taught Hebrew school for a number of years. Then I became--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first in Levittown for a number of years, I think, '61 to '60-- '64. And then '64, I got a job as a-- as a principal in the Jewish community of Princeton, New Jersey. And I was principal for two years, and then I got a position in, in Trenton in the reform synagogue for where I taught for-- where I was principal for eight years. And then I went on to Brothers of Israel, a conservative synagogue, for another eight years. So that's-- it all totals to about 24 years, teaching. And by the way, in those places, I definitely emphasized the Holocaust wherever I could. In fact, I introduced the program, the Holocaust. I mean, nowadays when you go to a religious school, there's no doubt about it that the Holocaust is being taught. But in those days, it was just-- it was just being known, just became a new thing. And I was one of the-- I feel I was one of the pioneers who introduced the Holocaust to, to the school and made-- and had my teachers teach it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in the Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hebrew school, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you incorporate any of the Holocaust in any of your history courses?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the public school?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes

speaker: interviewee
text: That was another thing. In those days, again, in those days, I would say the 1960s and '70s, again, the Holocaust was not emphasized as much. So first of all, when I taught history, I covered it all the time. And I tried to have all the other teachers work on it, but you can't force them. You have to, you know, use your psychology with it. But I was-- I was new at that, too. In other words, now, if you-- if you go back to Princeton High School, I'm pretty sure the Holocaust is being taught. But in those days, I had a rough time getting, getting the teachers to teach it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so do you feel that you started an awareness of the Holocaust in that area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely. I was one of those people who made these people aware that it should be taught. But it didn't become popular really until, until, until the end of 1980s, in other words, popular-- of course, Steven Spielberg's of course, definitely opened it up. But even before that, already there were some-- there were some changes and for the better. The state of New Jersey, for example, has a Holocaust program. The state of Pennsylvania has one. These were all done in the 1980s. So people were bringing it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you came down to Florida, was that in 1990?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come down to Florida?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you like it here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I like it here very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are some of the activities you're involved in here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first we have a, a clubhouse, a community-- a community here that has many of the programs. I belong to the Yiddish Club, which always has a good program. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Let me see what else. Oh, I teach in the elementary school in grade five in the public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a volunteer. I teach the fifth graders, and they're pretty good. And last year, in fact, I had a special assembly program for them to teach them about-- tell them about my experience and about the Holocaust. So I was telling them that I'm finally opening up. I've kept pretty quiet for years except, you know, making aware in some of the public schools. But now, after seeing Spielberg's I'm very glad I opened up. I finally opened up and decided to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that the children that you were teaching about the Holocaust, did you feel they were receptive to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I hope so. I mean, I didn't give a follow up-- follow up exam to them, but I would like to think that, yes, I, I, I made a-- I made an imprint. I made a dent in it, you know? I did something about it. And I'm going to do it again this year, at the end of the school year. Near the end of the school year, I'm going to have a special assembly and I'm going to bring up the Holocaust again to the new fifth graders. So I've been working on this, you know, and that's my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you feel it's important for you give your interview today and to be involved in these volunteer programs of teaching children about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the point is that I think the people need to know because I have met-- I have met, even my own colleagues back in Princeton High School, who say, did it really happen? So there are revisionists who are trying to say it did not happen, and I'm saying that it did happen. I was one of the many, many survivors and I don't know how they could, could even think otherwise. So I feel it's very important that we have this record and that we have proof and documentation that it did happen. So that's one of the basic reasons I'm doing it. And then the hate groups here in America, you know, there are still a lot-- a bunch of them that, whether they know it or not, they think it never happened either. And I think we've got to do something about it, and I think Steven Spielberg is doing the right thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you assess the prospects of a Nazi like takeover or influence like that in the American government in the near future might be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me put it this way, that I, I saw what happened in Germany. At the end of World War I, the people were so disgusted. First of all, they were in financial ruins, the German people. They were looking for a savior to get them out of this mess. Unfortunately, they looked to the wrong person, but Adolf Hitler was available. Now-- and Adolf Hitler was a demagogue. Now, in America, as long as we have the Constitution, as long as we have the Cons-- if nobody-- nobody touches the Bill of Rights, whether it's amendment one, amendment two, whatever, we're OK, I think. I feel comfortable that the Constitution will protect us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, unfortunately, there are many people in politics right now that want to change some of those amendments. And as far as I'm concerned, we better make sure that they don't. So remember, we have the militants in the Army, the ones that are not exactly-- are very militant. They're against government, anti-government and all that. You know what happened in Oklahoma City by these militar-- military people? We have, you know, what almost happened, Dave Duke in Louisiana? Thank goodness he didn't make it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, it's possible that the people may select the wrong-- may, may, may make the wrong decision about who their leaders are going to be. And I just hope to God that the people will, in the end, make the right decision.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Germany, pre-1933, have a constitution and a democracy at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany did not have the type of democracy that we have down here, here in America. Germany had a republic, but it was on a special type where it's on a party line or something-- proportional representation or something, where you vote for the party rather than for individuals. And it broke down, at least the German government broke down. I mean, the Nazis only had about 30% of the votes and yet, they were able to do so much damage because they got some other parties to go with them. And before you know, you had over 50% and were able to get their way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel your wartime experiences and pre-wartime experiences might have impacted either your religious philosophy or political philosophy? Let's take your religious philosophy first.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] let me think how I can word this. Why don't you say the religious and all--

speaker: interviewer
text: And political, why don't we take them all together. OK

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it has affected my feelings about eth-- ethnic cleansing, which is happenening-- happened in Bosnia. And definitely my experience has affected the way-- my thinking about it against genocide. I'm for education. I'm for, for world peace. I think what happened to [INAUDIBLE], by the way, is a tragedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all my f-- you know, everything is there. In other words, I f-- there are-- I think I feel that my political philosophy was really-- has really evolved from my experiences in the past. As you sit here today, do you have any message to anyone who might be viewing this, say either your grandchildren, your children, maybe your great-grandchildren, even anybody who might be viewing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the message would be that look where, where we're from, in other words, where we come from. I mean, our Jewish tradition and heritage is just outstanding, as far as I'm concerned, and we don't want it to, to disappear.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let's see what else? Jewish heritage. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Our beliefs in, in ethics-- in ethics, our belief to be concerned for others, our compassion for others, all these things are part of the Jewish religion. The belief in one God. Torah, you know? A lot of things that happen in the Torah that are, are very good, are very true. So I want my children to understand that there is a beautiful religion, and there are people on Earth that have made a contribution in music, in science, in art, in, in medicine, in politics, in psychology, that the world will never be able to take away. I mean, take whether it's Sigmund Freud or, or, or Jonas, Jonas Salk, or whatever, they made their contribution. I mean, there's a lot. You can give you a whole list of the Jewish contributions. So I am very-- I hope that they'll remember that. OK, well, thank you very much for coming today and giving us your testimony. Thank you very much. OK, Mr. Greenhut, can you tell us who this woman is that's sitting on your right?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lillian, and we've been married for 41 years, very happily.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've indicated you had three children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have three-- 13, 6, and 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mrs. Greenhut, let me ask you, was there a time that you went back to Europe with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we've been traveling in Europe for many, many years but then the German government invited us back to Karlsruhe in 1991, was it, Kurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1991, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had an opportunity to go through, to go to Karlsruhe again. It was a very, very difficult trip for us. And one of things we did was we were taken to the Jewish cemetery, which was behind very tall--

speaker: interviewee
text: Walls.

speaker: interviewee
text: --iron doors and walls. And with all the good records the Germans have kept, they told us where the plot was but we-- of Kurt's grandparents-- but we had a very difficult time nevertheless. They said it was something like the sixth grave in row two but there were all zigzags and we couldn't find it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I recognized it, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it took us-- and a lot of the lettering had been weathered away but we spent about an hour and a half there until we found the graves.

speaker: interviewee
text: See I read Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you Mr. Greenhut, what was your feelings about going back to Karlsruhe? And that was the first time you were back there since you had left there in 194--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my philosophy is very, very easy to understand, I hope to understand, but the young people, I do not hold them responsible for what their fathers have done. But when we were in Germany, unfortunately, I met a lot of these people that lived in my-- on my time, for example, you know, during 1930s. We met a lot of those people, and I was very nervous about it, very tight. But because as far as the young people are concerned, there's no problem with that. And I don't hold them responsible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the condition of the place that you had lived? Were you able to recognize it or the synagogue that you had alluded to that was destroyed on Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, we went back to [GERMAN] Strasse, where we-- where we left, and it wasn't there anymore because the Karl-- University of Karlsruhe exp, expanded into that area. So you don't see that area at all. Now, [GERMAN] Strasse is still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and that was the first place you had lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: My first place. Yeah, that's on the other side of Karlsruhe. But it disappeared. It's not there anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the synagogue that you had alluded to that was destroyed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they built a new one and we went there. And, you know, the German city officials immediately said, well, we would be interested in going to the synagogue, so we went to the synagogue. But what surprised me is that of the 180 Jews that are part of the synagogue, they were-- they were all Russian Jews. Only one, one pre-war German, and he didn't want to talk to me. He didn't want talk to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but you know who talked to me, was the, the guy who was protecting the synagogue. He was a former Nazi, and now he's protecting-- he's protecting the Jewish people. It was-- I couldn't believe it. You know, in my conversation with him in German, I realized that he was not Jewish. Then I checked the phone book and sure enough, it's not a Jewish name. It's Kiefer. Very interesting. OK, thank you very much. OK, Mr. Greenhut, can you tell us who's depicted in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father as a soldier in World War I. I guess he was born in 1890 so he must have been in the '20s when that picture was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. OK, Mr. Greenhut, can you tell us what this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the Iron Cross that all the Germans-- German soldiers got that were wounded and were brave and courageous in battle. This is how to win it if you were-- if you-- if you showed that type of courage and bravery.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you happen to come into possession of the Iron Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess he gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father gave it to me, yes, to carry it on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your father receive this Iron Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think near the end of the war, 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was when he was fighting for the Germans in World War I?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he wounded during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, twice. And he fought on both fronts, Eastern front and Western front. By the way, I hope you realize the Kaiser was not as anti-Semitic as Adolf Hitler You know, the Kaiser was quite a different personality.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much. Can you tell us who's depicted in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's-- excuse me. That's my mother on the left side and myself on the right-hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that taken approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Karlsruhe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. OK, Mr. Greenhut, can you tell us when this picture was taken and what it depicts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it had to be 1934 or '35 because some of these uncles, uncles left for New York, for the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's in this picture generally? Are these your relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the Greenhut family, the Greenhuts. This is all the Greenhut family.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the Greenhut family in Karlsruhe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anybody specifically that you could recognize and tell us who they are?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, my grandfather and grandmother are in the middle of the picture. And then my uncle-- my Uncle Sully is sitting in the same row as my grandparents. My Uncle Julius is standing in the end, in the back. And there's also my Uncle Albert over there on the left and, I think my, my Aunt Matilda is over there to the left. And I think we got the whole Greenhut, except for one, one person is missing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are the children that are sitting on the ground?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're all my cousins. They're all my cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the one that's not in the picture is my Uncle Emil, Emil. I don't know what happened to him. He's not there that picture, but everybody else in the family is.

speaker: interviewer
text: To your knowledge, is there anybody in that picture that died in the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you take away three-- my three uncles. I think most of them died in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. OK, Mr. Greenhut, can you tell us what this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is an ID card that every Jewish person in Germany received. It was 1938, and they gave us a middle initial. For the boys it was Israel, and for the girls it was Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every child had Israel as a middle name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel or Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your middle name was not Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had no middle name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much. OK, Mr. Greenhut, can you tell us what's depicted in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is the, the orthodox synagogue in Karlsruhe. They had two synagogues, and one was an orthodox synagogue. And, and below is the inside of the orthodox synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe a few months before the Crystal Night.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK thank you very much. OK, Mr. Greenhut, can you tell us what this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my moth-- when my mother came to the United States, she brought this piece of wood, which by the way, is a piece of wood that was-- she picked up from the burning synagogue. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this is the burning synagogue in the picture we had just seen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, and what happened is that we were thinking about making it some kind of a sculpture for it. And so, as you can see, the sculpture, it's like flames. Notice the flames? And it's very emotional for me. It means [NON-ENGLISH], which means 'thou shall not kill.' And it's been-- it's been really great to have this. It's wonderful. It reminds me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the synagogue burned down during Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Completely?

speaker: interviewee
text: Completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much

speaker: interviewee
text: This is important.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this thing? Yes, Mr. Greenhut, can you tell us what's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well this is the concentration camp in Gurs, and this is the main street here. And it looks like a, a truck or something on there, but notice the barracks. And this is what Gurs looked like. And Gurs, as you know, was divided up into sections called [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was the first camp you were in. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the first camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much. OK, Mr. Greenhut, can you tell us what's depicted in the top left picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's a group picture of some of the workers that, that were on the labor farm. After my father came back from, from Dachau, of course, he lost his job, you know? So he worked in the labor-- on the labor farm. And these are some of the-- in fact, you see a picture of me in there because I visited them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you're the young person on the bottom row there. And when was that taken approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it had to be around '39, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go over to the top right. Can you tell us what's in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of the Les Milles camp. It's a big, big building. That's all it is. A lot of people in there but it's not like the Gurs camp where they have barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, and when was that taken approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it had to be taken in 19-- 1941, somewhere in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let's go over to the bottom left. What's depicted in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're watching a soccer game. And notice, by the way, nobody was worried about what's going to happen in the future. Nobody knew at that time that-- what the Germans were going to do to us. So you can see, that we still think we're just a bunch of prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was approximately in 1940?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's depicted in the bottom right picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was at Quaker home for three months or so, these are some of the people that befriended me or I became friends with them. And these are some of the kids that we're in that-- all Spanish.

speaker: interviewer
text: All Spanish from the Civil War?

speaker: interviewee
text: Civil war, right, Spanish Civil War.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK thank you very much. Mr. Greenhut, can you describe, starting from the left, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my daughter Linda and she's holding Laura, her niece. And then next to her is Erin, that's her daughter. And then Scott, as you know, he's very tall.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and then my wife and then myself. And then my daughter-in-law with Diana. Diana at that time was young, very, very little. And then my son Stephen. And that's my family, and I hope it will grow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.
